meta.title: Solana Incubator: Grow Your Company Alongside Solana Labs

transcript: Thank you very much, Rebecca, for that great introduction. Hello Breakpoint, good afternoon. I am Emon Motamedi, I'm the head of the Solana Incubator within Solana Labs. You might be wondering, what is the Solana Incubator? Uh, what is Solana Labs potentially? As as many of you know, Solana Labs built a lot of the early products and protocols of the Solana network, the original validator client. But as the network has grown, as all of you amazing developers have come into the ecosystem, we asked ourselves, how can we scale the knowledge, the expertise, the resources we've built up to really help build the next blue chip companies on Solana? And the way that we decided to do that is through the Solana Incubator, where we work very closely with four to six teams max, um, to actually build alongside them, to to have this goal of really propelling them forward and being the next great company in in the ecosystem. We bring them to New York City for three months and have them work hands-on with with our team there. A lot of our alumni you're probably familiar with. You know, we were lucky enough to have, uh, Cohort 1, we had Sanctum, Espresso Cash, Shaga. Um, Cohort 2, we had teams like Marinade, Chakra, AlphaLedger, Easy Labs. Um, Cohort 3, we've had Pye, we've had Zynta, Genpulse. A lot of the teams you've probably seen, um, walking around here. They have booths, they have they've done talks. Um, and we actually just just did the math a few days ago. Seven of our teams, of the 18 teams that have gone through the program, have raised $5 million or more in funding so far, which is great. This also isn't including the teams that have been fully bootstrapped, you know, the the Marinades of the world, the Flash Trades of the world. And so we're we're really happy to see that. And so you might be sitting here wondering, hey, how can how can I have my company's name on that slide come next Breakpoint? Well, we opened up applications, uh, for Cohort 4 of the Incubator program. Um, they close a week from yesterday. And so if you're interested in coming and joining the Solana Labs team in New York starting in in March 2026 to to May 2026, definitely go ahead and submit an application. We also offer a relocation stipend to get folks to to New York and and be a part of it. Um, but let's dive into what the Incubator experience actually is. The the way that I think about it is it's it's three major components. The first component is the actual build alongside Solana Labs experience, where you have access to the full breadth of resources, the bench of uh personnel on the Solana Labs side, and I'll get into that in a second. The second is all of the traditional YC elements. So, you know, we have 90-plus events between office hours, between workshops, between speakers coming in. And so that's that's a core uh and integral part of each and every week there. And then the final part, and the part that maybe was most surprising to us, is the the camaraderie from with with the cohort itself, where at the end of every uh cohort, we hear from the teams that it really feels like a family being a part of this and and being able to work alongside these amazing founders. So we'll we'll talk about that a little bit too. So this this build alongside Solana Labs piece, what does that actually mean? Um, the first is this hands-on development piece. So we we sit down with every team on Monday morning for our weekly business review session to understand what are the major challenges facing the team in that week. So one week the team might say, we're looking to do our user experience design. And we'll have our head of design jump into their Figma, leave feedback, iterate on what that design could look like. Another week might be, hey, we're we're putting together our marketing strategy. We'll have head of marketing sit down with them on Monday, maybe we'll jump into a a Google Doc, leave comments on Tuesday. Hopefully by Wednesday actually be working toward executing that strategy and and and getting it out to the world. Fundraising support. So so whether it's um actually raising VC funding and us getting you in front of Solana Ventures or some of the other amazing VCs we have in the ecosystem. Whether it's uh something around Solana Foundation grants program. Whether it's um liquidity liquidity pools, market makers, etc. We try to get you in front of the right funding sources to make sure you're you're able to have the capital you need to help your business succeed. Um, this is a major one, strategic collaborations. So what we do at the start of every cohort is we ask each team, hey, if you could have a dream list of all of the people you could meet with during the course of this program, who would be on that list? Uh, and we actually go and we try to bring in, you know, 90-plus percent of the people you list out. You know, Cohort 2 we had one team list Nikita Bier as someone they really wanted to chat with, and we were actually able to get Nikita into the room. And then aligning product-market fit. I think it's so often in Web3 uh so so easy for us in Web3 to have our builder hat on all the time. But what does it actually mean to have the distribution hat to think through what are the right channels to get your product in? How can we use the Solana properties to help boost boost your brand, your your offering and get you in front of the right customers? Um, I know I mentioned all of the the events and office hours we have. You know, we've had the founders of Venmo, the founders of FanDuel, Raj and Toly, all of these amazing people come in to chat with the cohort where you can actually build relationships, collaborate with those folks. Um, here's just a a laundry list of of some of the folks we've had, but like I said, we've we've done 90-plus of these per per each of the cohort. We're we're also lucky to have some of the best uh founders in the Solana ecosystem mentoring each of our teams. Um, so the Jitos, Drifts of the world, etc. And then the cohort collaboration. So we've seen teams um uh partner with one another. We've seen founders join other other founders in their cohort. So to have Solana Labs, Anza, Foundation the floor above you, to have the ecosystem teams the floor uh below you, to be able to work back-to-back with some of the most amazing founders in in the ecosystem is really unparalleled. Um, and so with that, a call for Solana's most promising teams. You know, we're stage agnostic, we're vertical agnostic. We've we've helped early stage teams like a Sanctum before they became the the big thing they are today. And we've helped established teams like like a Marinade who's who's looking to push things to the next stage. And so if if you're hungry, would really encourage you to apply. Our application deadline once again is December 19th. Um, you can follow along @incubator, @EmonMotamedi. You can shoot us an email there. QR code leads to our website as well. And then to conclude, you know, if if you're the type of team who's sitting here thinking, hey, I really want to build the next big thing for the network. If you're a team who's thinking, I want to build a sustainable business, a brand that lasts the test of time. Um, if you want to have the most impactful three months of your company's existence, um, we would love to work with you to build the future of Web3 on Solana. Thank you very much.

sources.title: How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality | BAM
sources.snippet:
[Back to Blog](https://bam.dev/blog/)

November 12, 2025 • 8 minute read

# How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality

Lucas Bruder

Jito Labs

Share

- _Solana's long-term success depends on deterministic execution infrastructure that scales transparently as the network grows. BAM exists to ensure that as Solana scales, the execution layer remains transparent, fair, and aligned with the applications and users who drive network growth._
- _BAM provides cryptographically verifiable transparency for sequencing that no other solution can provide: every ordering decision proven, not promised._
- _BAM will help everyone using or building on Solana succeed:_
 - _Applications gain the execution certainty needed to offer tighter spreads and predictable fills through Application Controlled Execution (ACE)._
 - _Traders benefit from superior execution quality that rivals or exceeds other venues._
 - _Validators get open-source infrastructure they can verify. When applications succeed on Solana, validators succeed._

# What Is BAM?

Solana stands alone: the only blockchain where trading SOL is cheaper and faster on-chain than on centralized exchanges. With BAM, that lead widens as apps gain the tools to outprice and out-execute other exchanges. BAM provides the execution infrastructure necessary to make on-chain trading not just competitive with centralized venues, but categorically superior.

The Block Assembly Marketplace (BAM), which Jito Labs revealed in July, represents the evolution toward a more transparent and application-driven transaction processing paradigm. BAM addresses a fundamental challenge that has constrained the network's growth: the inability for applications to provide predictable, high-quality execution guarantees to their users.

# Why Solana's Block Building Needed to Evolve

We’ve detected at least seven different transaction schedulers operating on Solana, each with distinct ordering logic and timing characteristics. This fragmentation creates fundamental uncertainty for applications and traders who depend on predictable execution. Traders cannot determine whether they're receiving fair execution or being systematically disadvantaged by opaque scheduling decisions made by validators with competing interests. The result is wider spreads and reduced liquidity as market makers price in execution uncertainty, while developers face inconsistent environments that undermine their ability to compete with centralized alternatives.

# Why Jito Labs Is Building BAM

Jito Labs helped pioneer trading infrastructure on Solana through our Block Engine. While these systems created significant value for validators and improved transaction execution, we've also watched how they've evolved. We've seen concentration dynamics emerge, observed the pressure toward private orderflow relationships, and recognized that without intervention, Solana risks following the same path toward opacity that degraded Ethereum's execution layer.

Ethereum's experience with proposer-builder separation (PBS) offers particularly instructive lessons. While PBS introduced competitive block building, it created perverse incentives: builders maximize MEV extraction to win block auctions, often at users' expense. This dynamic leaves the network stuck in a positive feedback loop that can be hard to unwind. Increased sandwich attacks and other forms of extractive MEV pushed orderflow private, as users sought protection through direct builder relationships. Builders offered rebates for exclusive access to this orderflow, and those with the most private flow could pay higher auction fees, winning more blocks and attracting even more exclusive deals. The result is a concentration of power among a small number of builders, increased opacity in transaction processing, and timing games that degraded network performance. Most concerningly, some dominant builders were operated by trading firms with direct financial incentives to exploit

tags.impact_reason: Solana Incubator is a solid update for developers looking to build on Solana, providing resources and potential funding, but its impact is limited to a small number of teams.

These crystals pulse with a soft, internal light in Solana's signature purple and green hues. The background is a dark, out-of-focus, high-tech laboratory with clean lines and a subtle, cool-toned volumetric haze, creating a sense of depth and sterile focus.

Inside, from a dark, rich, organic substrate, intricate, complex glowing crystalline structures grow, representing incubated projects. These bioluminescent crystals pulse with a soft, internal light, shifting in vibrant Solana signature purple and luminous green hues, casting ethereal glows within the terrarium. Etched with razor precision onto the front glass panel, akin to a scientific classification, are the high-impact characters '$5M+', rendered as if deeply carved into the material itself, catching subtle reflections. The background is a dark, expansive, out-of-focus, high-tech laboratory environment, characterized by clean architectural lines and an overall cool-toned volumetric haze that creates a profound sense of sterile depth and focused isolation. Sharp focus on the terrarium, with a soft, cinematic bokeh background, enhanced by high dynamic range (HDR) and subtle chiaroscuro lighting to emphasize form and drama. The scene is bathed in sophisticated soft commercial product lighting for the terrarium, contrasting with volumetric lighting effects throughout the lab, creating a mysterious and advanced atmosphere. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, Unreal Engine 5.

Composed for a perfect square 1:1 aspect ratio, symmetrical, centrally focused, and tightly framed. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, ultra-photorealistic, V6 realism, Unreal Engine 5, cinematic golden-hour lighting, high dynamic range.

editorial.headline: Solana Labs Incubator Opens Cohort 4: 7 of 18 Alumni Raised Over $5M
editorial.standfirst: Solana Labs leverages its internal engineering and operational bench to mature the ecosystem with a high-touch NYC residency. The program’s alumni performance signals a increasingly reliable deal-flow pipeline for institutional investors.
key_takeaways: 7 of 18 alumni teams have secured $5M+ in funding, excluding bootstrapped successes.
Cohort 4 runs March–May 2026 in New York City; applications close December 19.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
Solana Labs is deepening its commitment to ecosystem maturity, transitioning from a pure protocol developer to a hands-on venture builder. Head of Solana Incubator **Emon Motamedi** revealed that the program is now accepting applications for its fourth cohort, slated for Spring 2026 in New York City. The incubator's value proposition is distinct in a crowded accelerator market: it offers direct access to the “bench” of **Solana Labs**—the same team that built the original validator client—to de-risk technical and operational hurdles for high-potential startups.

For investors, the program has emerged as a high-signal filter for quality. Motamedi disclosed that of the 18 teams to pass through the incubator so far, seven have raised $5 million or more. This statistic notably excludes successful bootstrapped projects like **Marinade** and **Flash Trade**, suggesting the actual success rate of the curriculum is even higher. Alumni include breakout protocols such as **Sanctum**, **Chakra**, and **AlphaLedger**, validating the incubator's ability to identify and scale “blue chip” companies across various stages of development.

The curriculum focuses heavily on bridging the gap between technical innovation and market distribution. Unlike standard hackathons, the incubator mandates a three-month relocation to NYC, facilitating weekly business reviews with Labs leadership. Teams receive granular support ranging from UX design in Figma to go-to-market strategy, alongside introductions to **Solana Ventures** and the **Solana Foundation** grants program. The mentorship roster is equally aggressive, featuring Web2 heavyweights like the founders of **Venmo** and **FanDuel**, as well as ecosystem leaders from **Jito** and **Drift**.

Applications for Cohort 4 are open until December 19, with the residency running from March to May 2026. The program is stage-agnostic, targeting both early-stage teams looking for product-market fit and established protocols like **Marinade** seeking to scale. By physically co-locating builders with **Anza**, the Foundation, and other ecosystem teams, Solana Labs is betting that proximity and institutional support will continue to drive the network's shift toward sustainable business models.
